m@berryhill: ~/berryhill.dev/about.md

m@berryhill in ~ $cat about.md
---
name: Matthew Berryhill
role: engineer + writer + RWA consultant
based: Austin, TX (GMT−5)
availability: selective consulting · 2 slots open Q3 2026
writing: 47 essays · 83 notes · weekly
focus: [agentic systems, tokenization, edge AI]
contact: matt@berryhill.dev
---
// $ whoami --verbose

I build agentic software. I write about it. I help capital markets people understand both.

Twelve years shipping infrastructure (Stripe, Compound, two acquired startups). Three years deep in agents, tokenization, and the awkward seam where AI labs and capital markets are slowly figuring out they need each other. I'm a generalist who can go deep — I'd rather be the person on the call who's read the SEC rule, written the Solidity contract, and used the agent framework all in the same week.

Years shipping
12
started as a backend eng, 2014
Companies
4
2 acquired · 1 wound down · 1 ongoing
Languages I ship in
TS · Go · Solidity · Python
+ enough Rust to read it
Open to talk about
advisory · writing · talks
not full-time roles
// table of contents
  1. What I actually do
  2. Things I believe
  3. How I got here
  4. How I work with people
  5. FAQ
  6. If you want to hire me

What I actually do

The honest answer is: three things, in different proportions every month.

Engineering. I write production code for two clients on retainer. One is a tokenized-treasury issuer; the other is a stealth-mode dev tools company building agent infrastructure. Combined that's roughly twenty-five hours of my week and accounts for most of my income.

Writing. About eight hours a week — one essay, two or three notes. The writing is not a marketing funnel. It's the way I figure out what I think. The essays I'm proudest of are the ones I wrote because the question wouldn't leave me alone, not the ones that performed.

Advising / consulting. Two or three calls a week with people building in adjacent spaces — usually capital markets infra, sometimes agent product teams, occasionally regulators. I keep this small on purpose. Most of the value I add is connecting people who should know each other, not the call itself.

Things I believe

Not load-bearing for the work, just a faster way to find out if we'd disagree before we waste a call.

01
Agentic development is real, and most of the discourse about it is downstream of vibes, not measurement. The teams shipping agentic features are running 50–200 iterations a week against their own evals. The teams arguing about whether agents work haven't built one. cf. the-agentic-dev-loop, the-non-coder-ceiling
02
Real-world asset tokenization is one of the two things crypto produces of net-positive value. The other is censorship-resistant payments. The rest is mostly noise. I came around to this slowly between 2022 and 2025. cf. rwa-tokenization-2026, where-the-smart-money-moved
03
The best engineers in five years will be defined by their taste in evals, not their typing speed. The agent writes the code; you decide whether the code is right. That's a different skill, and not everyone will make the transition cleanly.
04
Most consulting is repackaging. Mine isn't different. The value I add is reading three orders of magnitude more about a space than the client has time to, and giving them back the ten percent that matters.
05
Specificity is the cheapest leverage. A blog post that names the regulation, the case, and the dollar amount lands harder than ten essays of "things are changing." A pitch that names the customer, the contract, and the delivery date lands harder than ten about "the opportunity." cf. small-tools-big-leverage
06
Twitter is bad for thinking and good for distribution. Treat it accordingly. I draft on the page and post the conclusion, not the other way around.

How I got here

The short version, in case you're trying to decide whether I've seen what you've seen.

2024 — present
Independent · agentic systems + RWA consulting
berryhill.dev · Austin, TX
Two retained clients, advisory across six more. Writing weekly. Two side projects (see projects/).
2022 — 2024
Founding engineer · stealth (acquired)
on-chain settlement infra
Built the matching engine, the on-ramp, and most of the API surface. Sold to a public-markets infrastructure company at the end of 2024. Two-year cliff served; left when integration started.
2020 — 2022
Senior engineer · Compound Labs
DeFi protocol team
Worked on the v3 contracts and the off-chain risk engine. Co-authored the Compound rate model paper. This is where I started taking capital markets seriously as a domain.
2017 — 2020
Engineer · Stripe
Connect platform
Started on the payouts team, moved to Connect. Learned more about money movement, KYC, and ledger correctness here than at any other job before or since.
2014 — 2017
First eng · acq'd analytics startup
YC W15
First job out of school. Wrote almost the entire backend in Go. Sold the company to a bigger analytics vendor; rolled equity, stayed twelve months, left.

How I work with people

The shape of every engagement I take is roughly the same:

  • Diagnose, then quote. One free 30-minute call, then a one-page scoping memo with a fixed price for the next 4–8 weeks. I don't bill hourly anymore. Why.
  • One async update a week, one live call a fortnight. If you need me on Slack all day, I'm not the right person.
  • Source-available deliverables. Code I write for you is yours. Memos and reports I write for you are yours. The framing I use to write them is mine and shows up in the blog later, in disguised form.
  • I bring my own tools. Don't worry about a laptop budget or a Notion seat. I'd rather match your stack and write notes in plaintext.
  • I don't sign exclusivity. I'll happily sign an NDA and a non-compete for the duration of the engagement, but I won't agree to not work with anyone in your sector. The pattern recognition is the value.
The clients I do my best work for are the ones who treat me like a contractor with strong opinions, not a contractor who needs supervision. written on a Sunday, meant kindly

FAQ

The five questions I get every month.

Are you available for a full-time role?

No. Independent has been the best four years of my career and I have no intention of going back. If you're hiring and you think I'd be perfect, that's flattering, and the answer is still no.

There's exactly one exception: founding role at a capital markets x agent infra company, pre-seed, with co-founders I'd want to be in a foxhole with. If you think that's you, my inbox is open.

Will you ghostwrite or co-author for my company blog?

For one client at a time, yes. Rates are well above what a freelance writer would charge — closer to consulting rates — because I'm bringing the analysis as well as the prose.

I don't ghostwrite for crypto projects whose token I don't understand, AI products that are mostly wrappers, or anyone in defense.

Can I send you a deck for feedback?

Sure. I read everything I'm sent. I reply within a week, briefly, with the two things that struck me hardest — usually one positive and one harder. I don't do detailed page-by-page reviews unless we're working together.

Do you take equity instead of cash?

Sometimes — for advisory work with founders I trust at companies whose thesis I believe in. The minimum cash component is roughly half the dollar amount of a normal engagement.

I've passed on more equity offers than I've accepted. I'd rather be honest about that than soft about it.

What's your stack? What tools do you actually use?

Long-form answer at ~/uses.md. Short answer: Typescript, Go, and Solidity for shipping; Helix and tmux for editing; Claude for the agent loop. No Cursor, no Copilot. The reasons are in the linked file.

If you want to hire me

Email is best. Subject line: what you want done, in five words or fewer. Body: a paragraph on the problem, a sentence on what success looks like, and a date by which a decision matters. That's enough to get me to reply within a few days.

I'm not on Slack Connect, Discord, or WhatsApp for client comms. I won't get on a call until I've read your problem in writing. The asymmetric cost is on me — I can absorb a paragraph in two minutes; a 30-minute call costs us both an hour with prep and follow-up.

EMAIL matt@berryhill.dev best · 1–2 day reply CAL /matt/30min after we've written first LINKEDIN /in/mberryhill slow · don't dm here RSS /rss.xml subscribe to writing
